result:
:::writing
The night train doesn’t really arrive anywhere. It slows, it exhales, it pretends. Doors slide open onto platforms that look like every other platform—benches with peeling paint, a vending machine that hums like it knows a secret, a single person standing under a flickering light. You could step off, you could stay on. Either way, the train remembers you for exactly as long as it takes the doors to close.

Across from me, a man has been turning the same page of a book for an hour. Not reading—just touching the paper as if it might warm up and tell him something. When the conductor passes, the man looks up with a hopeful expression that never quite lands anywhere. The conductor punches his ticket anyway. Ritual matters more than belief on a night train.

Outside, the dark is layered. There’s the thin dark of passing fences, the thicker dark of trees, and beyond that a kind of patient dark that feels like it’s been waiting since before tracks were invented. Every now and then, a house appears—one window lit, a curtain breathing in and out. Someone inside is awake for reasons that have nothing to do with us. I like that. It makes the world feel less coordinated, more honest.

I’m carrying a small suitcase with a broken wheel. It complains softly whenever the train curves, a faint plastic whine. I haven’t opened it in days. I’m not entirely sure what I packed anymore. Clothes, probably. A book I meant to finish. A note I wrote to myself in a moment of certainty, sealed in an envelope like certainty could be preserved. I’m saving it for a stop that feels like an answer.

A woman gets on somewhere between here and there. She sits beside me without asking, as if the seat had been keeping her shape. We don’t speak. Our shoulders don’t touch, but they learn the same rhythm. After a while, she takes out a small orange and peels it in one long spiral. The scent is sudden and bright, like a memory you didn’t consent to having. She breaks off a segment and offers it without looking. I accept. It tastes like a morning I haven’t lived yet.

“Do you know where this goes?” she asks, finally.

“Not exactly,” I say. It feels like the right answer, even if it isn’t helpful.

She nods, satisfied in a way that makes me think she wasn’t asking for directions.

At the next almost-station, the man with the book stands up as if a decision has been made somewhere else and transmitted into his bones. He leaves the book on the seat, open to the page he’s been touching. I lean forward after he goes and read a single sentence before the doors close: “You can measure a life by the doors you didn’t walk through.” It feels like a dare and a comfort at the same time.

The train moves again. It always does. The woman beside me finishes the orange, folds the peel into itself like a small, careful map, and slips it into her pocket. “For later,” she says, though I didn’t ask.

We pass through a stretch where the tracks run close to water. The surface catches the train’s lights and returns them, broken and shimmering, as if trying to speak in a language made of reflections. I press my forehead to the glass. For a moment, I can see both myself and the night, superimposed—two versions of a person traveling in opposite directions.

“Next stop,” the conductor calls, though no one has told him what comes next.

The woman stands. “This might be mine,” she says, and smiles like someone who has decided to believe a small, useful lie.

“Good luck,” I say, because it’s the only blessing I know that doesn’t pretend to be more than it is.

She steps off. The doors close. The seat holds the shape of her for a second, then lets it go.

I reach down, open my suitcase, and find the envelope. The paper is softer than I remember. I tear it open and read the note I wrote to myself in a brighter hour: “You don’t need a destination to begin.” The ink has bled a little, as if the words have been traveling too.

The train hums, approving or indifferent—I can’t tell which. I fold the note and tuck it into my pocket, next to the future smell of oranges.

Outside, another almost-station appears, and the doors prepare to open on a place that might be real enough if I choose it. For the first time tonight, I stand before the train has fully stopped.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
